Q1: I had to be really intentional about my study routine because I'm 29 and juggling a lot more than the younger students. I blocked out about 39 hours a week for studying, mostly in the evenings after work and on weekends. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often record myself reading notes or listen to lecture recordings while commuting. The structure helped manage my anxiety, even though sticking to it was sometimes terrifying because I kept worrying I wasn't doing enough.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and life demands. I tried to keep some consistency, but honestly, when big assignments were due, everything else got pushed aside. Some weeks I'd be ahead of schedule, other weeks I'd be cramming at 2 AM feeling that familiar knot in my stomach. The irregular attendance—I know it was low—wasn't because I didn't care, but because work and personal obligations made it impossible to be there physically every time.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or the fear that I'd missed something important that got me to log in. I'd get this anxious feeling that everyone else knew something I didn't, so I'd check the LMS to calm myself down. Sometimes it was just to see if there were new announcements or if grades had been posted. That scared feeling was honestly my main motivator a lot of the time.
Q4: After the first month or so, logging in became almost automatic for me. I'd check it first thing in the morning with my coffee, just to see what was happening. The CBT sessions I attended helped me create better habits and routines, so making the LMS part of my daily check-in felt like a small win. It became a way to feel more in control when everything else felt overwhelming.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me—I watched over 265 minutes of them. As an auditory learner, being able to listen and re-listen to explanations made concepts stick in a way reading couldn't. The discussion boards were also helpful because seeing how peers interpreted things gave me different perspectives. I appreciated anything I could engage with through listening rather than just reading.
Q6: I'll admit I sometimes skipped over the supplementary readings or PDFs that seemed dense and text-heavy. My brain just doesn't absorb written material as well, and when anxiety was high, staring at walls of text made me feel more overwhelmed. I'd tell myself I'd come back to them, but often I'd find alternative audio or video resources instead. It wasn't laziness—it was survival.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams, or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. I also valued anything that reduced my anxiety about the course—like practice quizzes that showed me where I stood. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to my success, I'd prioritize other things. Time is precious when you're my age and managing multiple responsibilities.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a trap to me, honestly. Part of me wanted to do everything to feel prepared, but another part—the anxious part—worried I'd spread myself too thin and fail at the required stuff. Required activities had clear stakes, so I knew exactly what I was dealing with. Optional ones created this uncomfortable uncertainty about whether skipping them would come back to haunt me.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them during my commute or while doing chores. I also made audio recordings of my own notes and played them back repeatedly. My extracurricular commitments meant I had to be creative about when and where I studied, so a lot of it happened away from any screen the LMS could track.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd be on the bus with my earbuds in, listening to a lecture I'd downloaded days ago, really wrestling with the concepts. Or I'd be discussing ideas with a study group in person, working through problems together. Some of my most productive learning happened in spaces completely invisible to any digital tracking.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it or take detailed notes, and then work with that material offline for days. I couldn't always be connected, and honestly, being offline reduced some of my anxiety because I wasn't constantly tempted to check for new notifications or grades. One download might represent hours of subsequent engagement.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became really difficult, usually when my anxiety spiked. Midterms were brutal—I'd feel paralyzed by fear of failure even though I was putting in the work. There were also times when work got demanding and I felt stretched impossibly thin. During those moments, even logging into the LMS felt like facing something scary, so I'd avoid it until I absolutely couldn't anymore.
Q13: Fatigue and competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. When I was exhausted from work, I'd download what I needed and log off quickly rather than browsing or engaging with discussions. Confusion about course material sometimes made me avoid the LMS because I felt embarrassed to post questions. The workload was manageable most of the time, but when everything piled up at once, I had to triage ruthlessly.
Q14: There were times I'd have the LMS open but I was just going through the motions, not really absorbing anything. My eyes would scan the screen but my mind was elsewhere—worrying about an upcoming deadline or something happening at work. Sometimes I'd watch videos but realize I hadn't actually heard anything because anxiety was drowning everything out. Those moments felt like wasted time even though they'd show up as engagement.
Q15: I definitely postponed things, especially when they felt overwhelming or when I was scared of doing them wrong. Sometimes I'd avoid starting an assignment because the fear of failure was paralyzing. I knew it was important, but my brain would convince me I needed more preparation first. The CBT sessions helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was still a daily struggle.
Q16: Deadlines were like a double-edged sword for me. They created structure and forced me to act, which was necessary given my tendency to overthink. But they also triggered intense anxiety, especially as they approached. I'd often have a burst of LMS activity right before due dates—downloading materials, checking requirements obsessively, making sure I hadn't missed anything. Deadlines drove my engagement but also my stress.
Q17: Feedback had a huge impact on me, maybe too much. Positive feedback gave me a boost of confidence and made me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even when constructive, could send me into an anxiety spiral where I'd avoid the course for a bit while I processed it. I needed time to calm down before I could use feedback productively. The waiting for grades was often the most stressful part.
Q18: The course structure helped me know what to expect, which was essential for managing my anxiety. When modules were clearly laid out with specific deadlines, I could plan my 39 hours of study time effectively. But when things were ambiguous or changed unexpectedly, it threw me off completely. I relied heavily on predictability to feel safe enough to engage fully.
Q19: Having video content available was a huge help for my auditory learning style. The discussion forums were also good because I could engage with peers asynchronously, which worked better with my schedule. What made engagement harder was when important information was buried in long text documents or when navigation was confusing. Anything that added uncertainty or required extra effort to decode increased my anxiety and reduced my engagement.
Q20: I think that could reflect a lot of anxiety or confusion. Someone might be logging in repeatedly, watching videos, checking announcements—all because they're scared they're missing something or don't understand what's expected. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity. It could also be someone who's struggling to get started on actual work because they're overwhelmed by options or paralyzed by perfectionism.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My attendance was low and my login frequency wasn't exceptional, but I performed reasonably well because so much of my real learning happened offline. I downloaded materials and studied them intensively away from the LMS. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't engaged, but I was putting in nearly 40 hours a week—just not always in ways the system could see.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access—when you logged in, what you clicked, how long you stayed. They're decent at tracking completion of specific tasks. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, the emotional state behind the activity, and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture whether someone was actually absorbing material or just anxiously clicking through screens. They measure presence, not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, not just showing up. It's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, connecting them to things I already know, and feeling curious rather than just scared. Real engagement involves some level of investment—emotional, intellectual, and temporal. It's not about how many times I logged in; it's about whether the learning actually changed how I think or what I can do.
Q24: The LMS would never show the fear I felt almost constantly, or how much courage it took just to submit an assignment. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to recordings on the bus, or the peer discussions that happened over coffee. It wouldn't show that I'm 29, working while studying, and that every hour I gave to this course came at a real cost. The data shows actions, but not the person behind them or what those actions actually meant.
